Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:

Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.

Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.

# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

Wait, I see something interesting. The bad/bad.c already has `if (table == NULL)` at line 967? Let me check again...

Actually wait, looking at the grep results, line 967 (`if (table == NULL)`) is odd because that would be inside `decode_line_info`, not `concat_filename`. Let me check the actual content of line 967 in bad.c.

Actually, looking at the grep more carefully: the lines are 967 (`if (table == NULL)`), but that's likely inside decode_line_info for a different check. Let me look at the actual diff between bad and good more carefully.

Wait, I already have both files. Let me re-examine:

bad.c line 1448: `if (file - 1 >= table->num_files)`
good.c line 1448: `if (table == NULL || file - 1 >= table->num_files)`

So the fix in the test case is exactly what I identified: adding `table == NULL ||` to the condition. The baseline explanation is wrong because it says the issue is about `table->files` being NULL, when the actual fix is about `table` itself being NULL.

Now, the baseline also says "table->files pointer can be NULL if memory allocation for the file table fails or if initialization is incomplete, while table->num_files may still contain a non-zero value". This is speculative and doesn't match the actual fix.

The vastdb explanation correctly identifies that `table` (i.e., `unit->line_table`) can be NULL when `decode_line_info` was not invoked or failed, and that the dereference of `table->num_files` at line 1448 causes the NULL pointer dereference.

However, there's a subtle inaccuracy in vastdb's explanation: it says this happens via `scan_unit_for_symbols` calling `concat_filename` with `unit->line_table` when `decode_line_info` was never invoked. But looking at the code, `scan_unit_for_symbols` is always called AFTER `decode_line_info` succeeds. The trigger must involve `find_abstract_instance` changing the `unit` pointer when processing cross-CU references (DW_FORM_ref_addr), making `unit->line_table` point to a CU whose line_table hasn't been decoded yet.

But even with this slight imprecision, vastdb is much more accurate about the root cause than the baseline.

Let me now make my final judgement.

Baseline score: 2-3/10. It's incorrect about the root cause (focuses on `table->files` instead of `table`), although it does correctly identify the sink location (line 1448 in concat_filename) and recognizes it's a null pointer dereference.

VastDB score: 7-8/10. It correctly identifies that `table` (unit->line_table) can be NULL and the dereference at `table->num_files` is the issue. It correctly identifies the allocation source (`bfd_zalloc` at line 2160 zeroing the struct). The trigger path description has some minor imprecision but is largely on the right track.

Based on my analysis of the code, here's my explanation:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a Null Pointer Dereference in `concat_filename` (bad.c:1448). The `comp_unit` structure is allocated via `bfd_zalloc` at line 2160 in `parse_comp_unit`. Later, when `decode_line_info` creates the `line_info_table`, the `table->files` pointer can be NULL if memory allocation for the file table fails or if initialization is incomplete, while `table->num_files` may still contain a non-zero value. The check at line 1448 (`file - 1 >= table->num_files`) validates the file index against `num_files`, but does not check if `table->files` is NULL before dereferencing it at line 1457 (`filename = table->files[file - 1].name`). When a file index passes the bounds check but `table->files` is NULL, a null pointer dereference occurs.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:2258` `_bfd_dwarf2_find_nearest_line`
   - External entry point that initiates DWARF debug info processing

2. `bad.c:2294` (in `_bfd_dwarf2_find_nearest_line`)
   - Calls `_bfd_dwarf2_slurp_debug_info` which internally invokes `parse_comp_unit`

3. `bad.c:2038` `parse_comp_unit`
   - Called by the slurp function to parse compilation units

4. `bad.c:2160` `bfd_zalloc` in `parse_comp_unit`
   - Allocates the `struct comp_unit` - the source of the potential NULL if allocation fails

Based on my analysis of the code, here's my finding:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability (CWE-476 Null Pointer Dereference) occurs when `bfd_zalloc` allocates a `struct comp_unit` at line 2160, which zeroes all memory including the `line_table` pointer (set to NULL). Later, when functions like `scan_unit_for_symbols` call `concat_filename` with `unit->line_table` at lines 1682/1818/1873/1895, and if `decode_line_info` (line 1415) was never invoked or failed, the `line_table` remains NULL. At line 1448, accessing `table->num_files` on a NULL pointer triggers the null pointer dereference. The bounds check `file - 1 >= table->num_files` itself doesn't validate that `table` is non-NULL before dereferencing it.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:2294` `_bfd_dwarf2_slurp_debug_info`
   - Entry point that processes DWARF debug information from BFD.

2. `bad.c:2387` `comp_unit_find_line` (via loop at line 2382)
   - Iterates through all comp units to find line information.

3. `bad.c:2016` `comp_unit_find_line`
   - Calls `comp_unit_maybe_decode_line_info` to decode line info.

4. `bad.c:1982` `comp_unit_maybe_decode_line_info`
   - Calls `decode_line_info` to populate `unit->line_table` at line 1996.

946: static struct line_info_table*
947: decode_line_info (struct comp_unit *unit, struct dwarf2_debug *stash)
948: {
949:   bfd *abfd = unit->abfd;
950:   struct line_info_table* table;
951:   bfd_byte *line_ptr;
952:   bfd_byte *line_end;
953:   struct line_head lh;
954:   unsigned int i, bytes_read, offset_size;
955:   char *cur_file, *cur_dir;
956:   unsigned char op_code, extended_op, adj_opcode;
957:   unsigned int exop_len;
958:   bfd_size_type amt;
959: 
960:   if (! read_section (abfd, &stash->debug_sections[debug_line],
961: 		      stash->syms, unit->line_offset,
962: 		      &stash->dwarf_line_buffer, &stash->dwarf_line_size))
963:     return NULL;
964: 
965:   amt = sizeof (struct line_info_table);
966:   table = (struct line_info_table *) bfd_alloc (abfd, amt);
967:   if (table == NULL)
968:     return NULL;
969:   table->abfd = abfd;
970:   table->comp_dir = unit->comp_dir;
971: 
972:   table->num_files = 0;
973:   table->files = NULL;
974: 
975:   table->num_dirs = 0;
976:   table->dirs = NULL;
977: 
978:   table->num_sequences = 0;
979:   table->sequences = NULL;
980: 
981:   table->lcl_head = NULL;
982: 
983:   if (stash->dwarf_line_size < 16)
984:     {
985:       _bfd_error_handler
986: 	(_("DWARF error: line info section is too small (%" PRId64 ")"),
987: 	 (int64_t) stash->dwarf_line_size);
988:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
989:       return NULL;
990:     }
991:   line_ptr = stash->dwarf_line_buffer + unit->line_offset;
992:   line_end = stash->dwarf_line_buffer + stash->dwarf_line_size;
993: 
994:   /* Read in the prologue.  */
995:   lh.total_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
996:   line_ptr += 4;
997:   offset_size = 4;
998:   if (lh.total_length == 0xffffffff)
999:     {
1000:       lh.total_length = read_8_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1001:       line_ptr += 8;
1002:       offset_size = 8;
1003:     }
1004:   else if (lh.total_length == 0 && unit->addr_size == 8)
1005:     {
1006:       /* Handle (non-standard) 64-bit DWARF2 formats.  */
1007:       lh.total_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1008:       line_ptr += 4;
1009:       offset_size = 8;
1010:     }
1011: 
1012:   if (lh.total_length > (size_t) (line_end - line_ptr))
1013:     {
1014:       _bfd_error_handler
1015: 	/* xgettext: c-format */
1016: 	(_("DWARF error: line info data is bigger (%#" PRIx64 ")"
1017: 	   " than the space remaining in the section (%#lx)"),
1018: 	 (uint64_t) lh.total_length, (unsigned long) (line_end - line_ptr));
1019:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1020:       return NULL;
1021:     }
1022: 
1023:   line_end = line_ptr + lh.total_length;
1024: 
1025:   lh.version = read_2_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1026:   if (lh.version < 2 || lh.version > 5)
1027:     {
1028:       _bfd_error_handler
1029: 	(_("DWARF error: unhandled .debug_line version %d"), lh.version);
1030:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1031:       return NULL;
1032:     }
1033:   line_ptr += 2;
1034: 
1035:   if (line_ptr + offset_size + (lh.version >= 5 ? 8 : (lh.version >= 4 ? 6 : 5))
1036:       >= line_end)
1037:     {
1038:       _bfd_error_handler
1039: 	(_("DWARF error: ran out of room reading prologue"));
1040:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1041:       return NULL;
1042:     }
1043: 
1044:   if (lh.version >= 5)
1045:     {
1046:       unsigned int segment_selector_size;
1047: 
1048:       /* Skip address size.  */
1049:       read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1050:       line_ptr += 1;
1051: 
1052:       segment_selector_size = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1053:       line_ptr += 1;
1054:       if (segment_selector_size != 0)
1055: 	{
1056: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1057: 	    (_("DWARF error: line info unsupported segment selector size %u"),
1058: 	     segment_selector_size);
1059: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1060: 	  return NULL;
1061: 	}
1062:     }
1063: 
1064:   if (offset_size == 4)
1065:     lh.prologue_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1066:   else
1067:     lh.prologue_length = read_8_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1068:   line_ptr += offset_size;
1069: 
1070:   lh.minimum_instruction_length = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1071:   line_ptr += 1;
1072: 
1073:   if (lh.version >= 4)
1074:     {
1075:       lh.maximum_ops_per_insn = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1076:       line_ptr += 1;
1077:     }
1078:   else
1079:     lh.maximum_ops_per_insn = 1;
1080: 
1081:   if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 0)
1082:     {
1083:       _bfd_error_handler
1084: 	(_("DWARF error: invalid maximum operations per instruction"));
1085:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1086:       return NULL;
1087:     }
1088: 
1089:   lh.default_is_stmt = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1090:   line_ptr += 1;
1091: 
1092:   lh.line_base = read_1_signed_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1093:   line_ptr += 1;
1094: 
1095:   lh.line_range = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1096:   line_ptr += 1;
1097: 
1098:   lh.opcode_base = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1099:   line_ptr += 1;
1100: 
1101:   if (line_ptr + (lh.opcode_base - 1) >= line_end)
1102:     {
1103:       _bfd_error_handler (_("DWARF error: ran out of room reading opcodes"));
1104:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1105:       return NULL;
1106:     }
1107: 
1108:   amt = lh.opcode_base * sizeof (unsigned char);
1109:   lh.standard_opcode_lengths = (unsigned char *) bfd_alloc (abfd, amt);
1110: 
1111:   lh.standard_opcode_lengths[0] = 1;
1112: 
1113:   for (i = 1; i < lh.opcode_base; ++i)
1114:     {
1115:       lh.standard_opcode_lengths[i] = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1116:       line_ptr += 1;
1117:     }
1118: 
1119:   if (lh.version >= 5)
1120:     {
1121:       /* Read directory table.  */
1122:       if (!read_formatted_entries (unit, &line_ptr, line_end, table,
1123: 				   line_info_add_include_dir_stub))
1124: 	goto fail;
1125: 
1126:       /* Read file name table.  */
1127:       if (!read_formatted_entries (unit, &line_ptr, line_end, table,
1128: 				   line_info_add_file_name))
1129: 	goto fail;
1130:     }
1131:   else
1132:     {
1133:       /* Read directory table.  */
1134:       while ((cur_dir = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read)) != NULL)
1135: 	{
1136: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1137: 
1138: 	  if (!line_info_add_include_dir (table, cur_dir))
1139: 	    goto fail;
1140: 	}
1141: 
1142:       line_ptr += bytes_read;
1143: 
1144:       /* Read file name table.  */
1145:       while ((cur_file = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read)) != NULL)
1146: 	{
1147: 	  unsigned int dir, xtime, size;
1148: 
1149: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1150: 
1151: 	  dir = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1152: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1153: 	  xtime = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1154: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1155: 	  size = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1156: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1157: 
1158: 	  if (!line_info_add_file_name (table, cur_file, dir, xtime, size))
1159: 	    goto fail;
1160: 	}
1161: 
1162:       line_ptr += bytes_read;
1163:     }
1164: 
1165:   /* Read the statement sequences until there's nothing left.  */
1166:   while (line_ptr < line_end)
1167:     {
1168:       /* State machine registers.  */
1169:       bfd_vma address = 0;
1170:       unsigned char op_index = 0;
1171:       char * filename = table->num_files ? concat_filename (table, 1) : NULL;
1172:       unsigned int line = 1;
1173:       unsigned int column = 0;
1174:       unsigned int discriminator = 0;
1175:       int is_stmt = lh.default_is_stmt;
1176:       int end_sequence = 0;
1177:       unsigned int dir, xtime, size;
1178:       /* eraxxon@alumni.rice.edu: Against the DWARF2 specs, some
1179: 	 compilers generate address sequences that are wildly out of
1180: 	 order using DW_LNE_set_address (e.g. Intel C++ 6.0 compiler
1181: 	 for ia64-Linux).  Thus, to determine the low and high
1182: 	 address, we must compare on every DW_LNS_copy, etc.  */
1183:       bfd_vma low_pc  = (bfd_vma) -1;
1184:       bfd_vma high_pc = 0;
1185: 
1186:       /* Decode the table.  */
1187:       while (!end_sequence && line_ptr < line_end)
1188: 	{
1189: 	  op_code = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1190: 	  line_ptr += 1;
1191: 
1192: 	  if (op_code >= lh.opcode_base)
1193: 	    {
1194: 	      /* Special operand.  */
1195: 	      adj_opcode = op_code - lh.opcode_base;
1196: 	      if (lh.line_range == 0)
1197: 		goto line_fail;
1198: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1199: 		address += (adj_opcode / lh.line_range
1200: 			    * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1201: 	      else
1202: 		{
1203: 		  address += ((op_index + adj_opcode / lh.line_range)
1204: 			      / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn
1205: 			      * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1206: 		  op_index = ((op_index + adj_opcode / lh.line_range)
1207: 			      % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn);
1208: 		}
1209: 	      line += lh.line_base + (adj_opcode % lh.line_range);
1210: 	      /* Append row to matrix using current values.  */
1211: 	      if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index, filename,
1212: 				  line, column, discriminator, 0))
1213: 		goto line_fail;
1214: 	      discriminator = 0;
1215: 	      if (address < low_pc)
1216: 		low_pc = address;
1217: 	      if (address > high_pc)
1218: 		high_pc = address;
1219: 	    }
1220: 	  else switch (op_code)
1221: 	    {
1222: 	    case DW_LNS_extended_op:
1223: 	      exop_len = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1224: 						FALSE, line_end);
1225: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1226: 	      extended_op = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1227: 	      line_ptr += 1;
1228: 
1229: 	      switch (extended_op)
1230: 		{
1231: 		case DW_LNE_end_sequence:
1232: 		  end_sequence = 1;
1233: 		  if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index, filename, line,
1234: 				      column, discriminator, end_sequence))
1235: 		    goto line_fail;
1236: 		  discriminator = 0;
1237: 		  if (address < low_pc)
1238: 		    low_pc = address;
1239: 		  if (address > high_pc)
1240: 		    high_pc = address;
1241: 		  if (!arange_add (unit, &unit->arange, low_pc, high_pc))
1242: 		    goto line_fail;
1243: 		  break;
1244: 		case DW_LNE_set_address:
1245: 		  address = read_address (unit, line_ptr, line_end);
1246: 		  op_index = 0;
1247: 		  line_ptr += unit->addr_size;
1248: 		  break;
1249: 		case DW_LNE_define_file:
1250: 		  cur_file = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read);
1251: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1252: 		  dir = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1253: 					       FALSE, line_end);
1254: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1255: 		  xtime = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1256: 						 FALSE, line_end);
1257: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1258: 		  size = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1259: 						FALSE, line_end);
1260: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1261: 		  if (!line_info_add_file_name (table, cur_file, dir,
1262: 						xtime, size))
1263: 		    goto line_fail;
1264: 		  break;
1265: 		case DW_LNE_set_discriminator:
1266: 		  discriminator =
1267: 		    _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1268: 					   FALSE, line_end);
1269: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1270: 		  break;
1271: 		case DW_LNE_HP_source_file_correlation:
1272: 		  line_ptr += exop_len - 1;
1273: 		  break;
1274: 		default:
1275: 		  _bfd_error_handler
1276: 		    (_("DWARF error: mangled line number section"));
1277: 		  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1278: 		line_fail:
1279: 		  if (filename != NULL)
1280: 		    free (filename);
1281: 		  goto fail;
1282: 		}
1283: 	      break;
1284: 	    case DW_LNS_copy:
1285: 	      if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index,
1286: 				  filename, line, column, discriminator, 0))
1287: 		goto line_fail;
1288: 	      discriminator = 0;
1289: 	      if (address < low_pc)
1290: 		low_pc = address;
1291: 	      if (address > high_pc)
1292: 		high_pc = address;
1293: 	      break;
1294: 	    case DW_LNS_advance_pc:
1295: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1296: 		address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1297: 			    * _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr,
1298: 						     &bytes_read,
1299: 						     FALSE, line_end));
1300: 	      else
1301: 		{
1302: 		  bfd_vma adjust = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr,
1303: 							  &bytes_read,
1304: 							  FALSE, line_end);
1305: 		  address = ((op_index + adjust) / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn
1306: 			     * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1307: 		  op_index = (op_index + adjust) % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn;
1308: 		}
1309: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1310: 	      break;
1311: 	    case DW_LNS_advance_line:
1312: 	      line += _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1313: 					     TRUE, line_end);
1314: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1315: 	      break;
1316: 	    case DW_LNS_set_file:
1317: 	      {
1318: 		unsigned int file;
1319: 
1320: 		/* The file and directory tables are 0
1321: 		   based, the references are 1 based.  */
1322: 		file = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1323: 					      FALSE, line_end);
1324: 		line_ptr += bytes_read;
1325: 		if (filename)
1326: 		  free (filename);
1327: 		filename = concat_filename (table, file);
1328: 		break;
1329: 	      }
1330: 	    case DW_LNS_set_column:
1331: 	      column = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1332: 					      FALSE, line_end);
1333: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1334: 	      break;
1335: 	    case DW_LNS_negate_stmt:
1336: 	      is_stmt = (!is_stmt);
1337: 	      break;
1338: 	    case DW_LNS_set_basic_block:
1339: 	      break;
1340: 	    case DW_LNS_const_add_pc:
1341: 	      if (lh.line_range == 0)
1342: 		goto line_fail;
1343: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1344: 		address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1345: 			    * ((255 - lh.opcode_base) / lh.line_range));
1346: 	      else
1347: 		{
1348: 		  bfd_vma adjust = ((255 - lh.opcode_base) / lh.line_range);
1349: 		  address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1350: 			      * ((op_index + adjust)
1351: 				 / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn));
1352: 		  op_index = (op_index + adjust) % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn;
1353: 		}
1354: 	      break;
1355: 	    case DW_LNS_fixed_advance_pc:
1356: 	      address += read_2_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1357: 	      op_index = 0;
1358: 	      line_ptr += 2;
1359: 	      break;
1360: 	    default:
1361: 	      /* Unknown standard opcode, ignore it.  */
1362: 	      for (i = 0; i < lh.standard_opcode_lengths[op_code]; i++)
1363: 		{
1364: 		  (void) _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1365: 						FALSE, line_end);
1366: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1367: 		}
1368: 	      break;
1369: 	    }
1370: 	}
1371: 
1372:       if (filename)
1373: 	free (filename);
1374:     }
1375: 
1376:   if (sort_line_sequences (table))
1377:     return table;
1378: 
1379:  fail:
1380:   while (table->sequences != NULL)
1381:     {
1382:       struct line_sequence* seq = table->sequences;
1383:       table->sequences = table->sequences->prev_sequence;
1384:       free (seq);
1385:     }
1386:   if (table->files != NULL)
1387:     free (table->files);
1388:   if (table->dirs != NULL)
1389:     free (table->dirs);
1390:   return NULL;
1391: }

1393: static bfd_vma
1394: comp_unit_find_nearest_line (struct comp_unit *unit,
1395: 			     bfd_vma addr,
1396: 			     const char **filename_ptr,
1397: 			     struct funcinfo **function_ptr,
1398: 			     unsigned int *linenumber_ptr,
1399: 			     unsigned int *discriminator_ptr,
1400: 			     struct dwarf2_debug *stash)
1401: {
1402:   bfd_boolean func_p;
1403: 
1404:   if (unit->error)
1405:     return FALSE;
1406: 
1407:   if (! unit->line_table)
1408:     {
1409:       if (! unit->stmtlist)
1410: 	{
1411: 	  unit->error = 1;
1412: 	  return FALSE;
1413: 	}
1414: 
1415:       unit->line_table = decode_line_info (unit, stash);
1416: 
1417:       if (! unit->line_table)
1418: 	{
1419: 	  unit->error = 1;
1420: 	  return FALSE;
1421: 	}
1422: 
1423:       if (unit->first_child_die_ptr < unit->end_ptr
1424: 	  && ! scan_unit_for_symbols (unit))
1425: 	{
1426: 	  unit->error = 1;
1427: 	  return FALSE;
1428: 	}
1429:     }
1430: 
1431:   *function_ptr = NULL;
1432:   func_p = lookup_address_in_function_table (unit, addr, function_ptr);
1433:   if (func_p && (*function_ptr)->tag == DW_TAG_inlined_subroutine)
1434:     stash->inliner_chain = *function_ptr;
1435: 
1436:   return lookup_address_in_line_info_table (unit->line_table, addr,
1437: 					    filename_ptr,
1438: 					    linenumber_ptr,
1439: 					    discriminator_ptr);
1440: }

1443: static char *
1444: concat_filename (struct line_info_table *table, unsigned int file)
1445: {
1446:   char *filename;
1447: 
1448:   if (file - 1 >= table->num_files)
1449:     {
1450:       /* FILE == 0 means unknown.  */
1451:       if (file)
1452: 	_bfd_error_handler
1453: 	  (_("DWARF error: mangled line number section (bad file number)"));
1454:       return strdup ("<unknown>");
1455:     }
1456: 
1457:   filename = table->files[file - 1].name;
1458:   if (filename == NULL)
1459:     return strdup ("<unknown>");
1460: 
1461:   if (!IS_ABSOLUTE_PATH (filename))
1462:     {
1463:       char *dir_name = NULL;
1464:       char *subdir_name = NULL;
1465:       char *name;
1466:       size_t len;
1467: 
1468:       if (table->files[file - 1].dir
1469: 	  /* PR 17512: file: 0317e960.  */
1470: 	  && table->files[file - 1].dir <= table->num_dirs
1471: 	  /* PR 17512: file: 7f3d2e4b.  */
1472: 	  && table->dirs != NULL)
1473: 	subdir_name = table->dirs[table->files[file - 1].dir - 1];
1474: 
1475:       if (!subdir_name || !IS_ABSOLUTE_PATH (subdir_name))
1476: 	dir_name = table->comp_dir;
1477: 
1478:       if (!dir_name)
1479: 	{
1480: 	  dir_name = subdir_name;
1481: 	  subdir_name = NULL;
1482: 	}
1483: 
1484:       if (!dir_name)
1485: 	return strdup (filename);
1486: 
1487:       len = strlen (dir_name) + strlen (filename) + 2;
1488: 
1489:       if (subdir_name)
1490: 	{
1491: 	  len += strlen (subdir_name) + 1;
1492: 	  name = (char *) bfd_malloc (len);
1493: 	  if (name)
1494: 	    sprintf (name, "%s/%s/%s", dir_name, subdir_name, filename);
1495: 	}
1496:       else
1497: 	{
1498: 	  name = (char *) bfd_malloc (len);
1499: 	  if (name)
1500: 	    sprintf (name, "%s/%s", dir_name, filename);
1501: 	}
1502: 
1503:       return name;
1504:     }
1505: 
1506:   return strdup (filename);
1507: }

1509: static bfd_boolean
1510: find_abstract_instance (struct comp_unit *   unit,
1511: 			bfd_byte *           orig_info_ptr,
1512: 			struct attribute *   attr_ptr,
1513: 			const char **        pname,
1514: 			bfd_boolean *        is_linkage,
1515: 			char **              filename_ptr,
1516: 			int *                linenumber_ptr)
1517: {
1518:   bfd *abfd = unit->abfd;
1519:   bfd_byte *info_ptr;
1520:   bfd_byte *info_ptr_end;
1521:   unsigned int abbrev_number, bytes_read, i;
1522:   struct abbrev_info *abbrev;
1523:   bfd_uint64_t die_ref = attr_ptr->u.val;
1524:   struct attribute attr;
1525:   const char *name = NULL;
1526: 
1527:   /* DW_FORM_ref_addr can reference an entry in a different CU. It
1528:      is an offset from the .debug_info section, not the current CU.  */
1529:   if (attr_ptr->form == DW_FORM_ref_addr)
1530:     {
1531:       /* We only support DW_FORM_ref_addr within the same file, so
1532: 	 any relocations should be resolved already.  Check this by
1533: 	 testing for a zero die_ref;  There can't be a valid reference
1534: 	 to the header of a .debug_info section.
1535: 	 DW_FORM_ref_addr is an offset relative to .debug_info.
1536: 	 Normally when using the GNU linker this is accomplished by
1537: 	 emitting a symbolic reference to a label, because .debug_info
1538: 	 sections are linked at zero.  When there are multiple section
1539: 	 groups containing .debug_info, as there might be in a
1540: 	 relocatable object file, it would be reasonable to assume that
1541: 	 a symbolic reference to a label in any .debug_info section
1542: 	 might be used.  Since we lay out multiple .debug_info
1543: 	 sections at non-zero VMAs (see place_sections), and read
1544: 	 them contiguously into stash->info_ptr_memory, that means
1545: 	 the reference is relative to stash->info_ptr_memory.  */
1546:       size_t total;
1547: 
1548:       info_ptr = unit->stash->info_ptr_memory;
1549:       info_ptr_end = unit->stash->info_ptr_end;
1550:       total = info_ptr_end - info_ptr;
1551:       if (!die_ref || die_ref >= total)
1552: 	{
1553: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1554: 	    (_("DWARF error: invalid abstract instance DIE ref"));
1555: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1556: 	  return FALSE;
1557: 	}
1558:       info_ptr += die_ref;
1559: 
1560:       /* Now find the CU containing this pointer.  */
1561:       if (info_ptr >= unit->info_ptr_unit && info_ptr < unit->end_ptr)
1562: 	info_ptr_end = unit->end_ptr;
1563:       else
1564: 	{
1565: 	  /* Check other CUs to see if they contain the abbrev.  */
1566: 	  struct comp_unit * u;
1567: 
1568: 	  for (u = unit->prev_unit; u != NULL; u = u->prev_unit)
1569: 	    if (info_ptr >= u->info_ptr_unit && info_ptr < u->end_ptr)
1570: 	      break;
1571: 
1572: 	  if (u == NULL)
1573: 	    for (u = unit->next_unit; u != NULL; u = u->next_unit)
1574: 	      if (info_ptr >= u->info_ptr_unit && info_ptr < u->end_ptr)
1575: 		break;
1576: 
1577: 	  if (u)
1578: 	    {
1579: 	      unit = u;
1580: 	      info_ptr_end = unit->end_ptr;
1581: 	    }
1582: 	  /* else FIXME: What do we do now ?  */
1583: 	}
1584:     }
1585:   else if (attr_ptr->form == DW_FORM_GNU_ref_alt)
1586:     {
1587:       info_ptr = read_alt_indirect_ref (unit, die_ref);
1588:       if (info_ptr == NULL)
1589: 	{
1590: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1591: 	    (_("DWARF error: unable to read alt ref %" PRIu64),
1592: 	     (uint64_t) die_ref);
1593: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1594: 	  return FALSE;
1595: 	}
1596:       info_ptr_end = (unit->stash->alt_dwarf_info_buffer
1597: 		      + unit->stash->alt_dwarf_info_size);
1598: 
1599:       /* FIXME: Do we need to locate the correct CU, in a similar
1600: 	 fashion to the code in the DW_FORM_ref_addr case above ?  */
1601:     }
1602:   else
1603:     {
1604:       /* DW_FORM_ref1, DW_FORM_ref2, DW_FORM_ref4, DW_FORM_ref8 or
1605: 	 DW_FORM_ref_udata.  These are all references relative to the
1606: 	 start of the current CU.  */
1607:       size_t total;
1608: 
1609:       info_ptr = unit->info_ptr_unit;
1610:       info_ptr_end = unit->end_ptr;
1611:       total = info_ptr_end - info_ptr;
1612:       if (!die_ref || die_ref >= total)
1613: 	{
1614: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1615: 	    (_("DWARF error: invalid abstract instance DIE ref"));
1616: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1617: 	  return FALSE;
1618: 	}
1619:       info_ptr += die_ref;
1620:     }
1621: 
1622:   abbrev_number = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, info_ptr, &bytes_read,
1623: 					 FALSE, info_ptr_end);
1624:   info_ptr += bytes_read;
1625: 
1626:   if (abbrev_number)
1627:     {
1628:       abbrev = lookup_abbrev (abbrev_number, unit->abbrevs);
1629:       if (! abbrev)
1630: 	{
1631: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1632: 	    (_("DWARF error: could not find abbrev number %u"), abbrev_number);
1633: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1634: 	  return FALSE;
1635: 	}
1636:       else
1637: 	{
1638: 	  for (i = 0; i < abbrev->num_attrs; ++i)
1639: 	    {
1640: 	      info_ptr = read_attribute (&attr, &abbrev->attrs[i], unit,
1641: 					 info_ptr, info_ptr_end);
1642: 	      if (info_ptr == NULL)
1643: 		break;
1644: 	      /* It doesn't ever make sense for DW_AT_specification to
1645: 		 refer to the same DIE.  Stop simple recursion.  */
1646: 	      if (info_ptr == orig_info_ptr)
1647: 		{
1648: 		  _bfd_error_handler
1649: 		    (_("DWARF error: abstract instance recursion detected"));
1650: 		  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1651: 		  return FALSE;
1652: 		}
1653: 	      switch (attr.name)
1654: 		{
1655: 		case DW_AT_name:
1656: 		  /* Prefer DW_AT_MIPS_linkage_name or DW_AT_linkage_name
1657: 		     over DW_AT_name.  */
1658: 		  if (name == NULL && is_str_attr (attr.form))
1659: 		    {
1660: 		      name = attr.u.str;
1661: 		      if (non_mangled (unit->lang))
1662: 			*is_linkage = TRUE;
1663: 		    }
1664: 		  break;
1665: 		case DW_AT_specification:
1666: 		  if (!find_abstract_instance (unit, info_ptr, &attr,
1667: 					       pname, is_linkage,
1668: 					       filename_ptr, linenumber_ptr))
1669: 		    return FALSE;
1670: 		  break;
1671: 		case DW_AT_linkage_name:
1672: 		case DW_AT_MIPS_linkage_name:
1673: 		  /* PR 16949:  Corrupt debug info can place
1674: 		     non-string forms into these attributes.  */
1675: 		  if (is_str_attr (attr.form))
1676: 		    {
1677: 		      name = attr.u.str;
1678: 		      *is_linkage = TRUE;
1679: 		    }
1680: 		  break;
1681: 		case DW_AT_decl_file:
1682: 		  *filename_ptr = concat_filename (unit->line_table,
1683: 						   attr.u.val);
1684: 		  break;
1685: 		case DW_AT_decl_line:
1686: 		  *linenumber_ptr = attr.u.val;
1687: 		  break;
1688: 		default:
1689: 		  break;
1690: 		}
1691: 	    }
1692: 	}
1693:     }
1694:   *pname = name;
1695:   return TRUE;
1696: }

1698: static bfd_boolean
1699: scan_unit_for_symbols (struct comp_unit *unit)
1700: {
1701:   bfd *abfd = unit->abfd;
1702:   bfd_byte *info_ptr = unit->first_child_die_ptr;
1703:   bfd_byte *info_ptr_end = unit->stash->info_ptr_end;
1704:   int nesting_level = 0;
1705:   struct nest_funcinfo {
1706:     struct funcinfo *func;
1707:   } *nested_funcs;
1708:   int nested_funcs_size;
1709: 
1710:   /* Maintain a stack of in-scope functions and inlined functions, which we
1711:      can use to set the caller_func field.  */
1712:   nested_funcs_size = 32;
1713:   nested_funcs = (struct nest_funcinfo *)
1714:     bfd_malloc (nested_funcs_size * sizeof (*nested_funcs));
1715:   if (nested_funcs == NULL)
1716:     return FALSE;
1717:   nested_funcs[nesting_level].func = 0;
1718: 
1719:   while (nesting_level >= 0)
1720:     {
1721:       unsigned int abbrev_number, bytes_read, i;
1722:       struct abbrev_info *abbrev;
1723:       struct attribute attr;
1724:       struct funcinfo *func;
1725:       struct varinfo *var;
1726:       bfd_vma low_pc = 0;
1727:       bfd_vma high_pc = 0;
1728:       bfd_boolean high_pc_relative = FALSE;
1729: 
1730:       /* PR 17512: file: 9f405d9d.  */
1731:       if (info_ptr >= info_ptr_end)
1732: 	goto fail;
1733: 
1734:       abbrev_number = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, info_ptr, &bytes_read,
1735: 					     FALSE, info_ptr_end);
1736:       info_ptr += bytes_read;
1737: 
1738:       if (! abbrev_number)
1739: 	{
1740: 	  nesting_level--;
1741: 	  continue;
1742: 	}
1743: 
1744:       abbrev = lookup_abbrev (abbrev_number, unit->abbrevs);
1745:       if (! abbrev)
1746: 	{
1747: 	  static unsigned int previous_failed_abbrev = -1U;
1748: 
1749: 	  /* Avoid multiple reports of the same missing abbrev.  */
1750: 	  if (abbrev_number != previous_failed_abbrev)
1751: 	    {
1752: 	      _bfd_error_handler
1753: 		(_("DWARF error: could not find abbrev number %u"),
1754: 		 abbrev_number);
1755: 	      previous_failed_abbrev = abbrev_number;

970:   table->comp_dir = unit->comp_dir;
971: 
972:   table->num_files = 0;
973:   table->files = NULL;
974: 
975:   table->num_dirs = 0;
976:   table->dirs = NULL;
977: 
978:   table->num_sequences = 0;
979:   table->sequences = NULL;
980: 
981:   table->lcl_head = NULL;
982: 
983:   if (stash->dwarf_line_size < 16)
984:     {
985:       _bfd_error_handler
986: 	(_("DWARF error: line info section is too small (%" PRId64 ")"),
987: 	 (int64_t) stash->dwarf_line_size);
988:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
989:       return NULL;
990:     }
991:   line_ptr = stash->dwarf_line_buffer + unit->line_offset;
992:   line_end = stash->dwarf_line_buffer + stash->dwarf_line_size;
993: 
994:   /* Read in the prologue.  */
995:   lh.total_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
996:   line_ptr += 4;
997:   offset_size = 4;
998:   if (lh.total_length == 0xffffffff)
999:     {
1000:       lh.total_length = read_8_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1001:       line_ptr += 8;
1002:       offset_size = 8;
1003:     }
1004:   else if (lh.total_length == 0 && unit->addr_size == 8)
1005:     {
1006:       /* Handle (non-standard) 64-bit DWARF2 formats.  */
1007:       lh.total_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1008:       line_ptr += 4;
1009:       offset_size = 8;
1010:     }
1011: 
1012:   if (lh.total_length > (size_t) (line_end - line_ptr))
1013:     {
1014:       _bfd_error_handler
1015: 	/* xgettext: c-format */
1016: 	(_("DWARF error: line info data is bigger (%#" PRIx64 ")"
1017: 	   " than the space remaining in the section (%#lx)"),
1018: 	 (uint64_t) lh.total_length, (unsigned long) (line_end - line_ptr));
1019:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1020:       return NULL;
1021:     }
1022: 
1023:   line_end = line_ptr + lh.total_length;
1024: 
1025:   lh.version = read_2_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1026:   if (lh.version < 2 || lh.version > 5)
1027:     {
1028:       _bfd_error_handler
1029: 	(_("DWARF error: unhandled .debug_line version %d"), lh.version);
1030:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1031:       return NULL;
1032:     }
1033:   line_ptr += 2;
1034: 
1035:   if (line_ptr + offset_size + (lh.version >= 5 ? 8 : (lh.version >= 4 ? 6 : 5))
1036:       >= line_end)
1037:     {
1038:       _bfd_error_handler
1039: 	(_("DWARF error: ran out of room reading prologue"));
1040:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1041:       return NULL;
1042:     }
1043: 
1044:   if (lh.version >= 5)
1045:     {
1046:       unsigned int segment_selector_size;
1047: 
1048:       /* Skip address size.  */
1049:       read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1050:       line_ptr += 1;
1051: 
1052:       segment_selector_size = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1053:       line_ptr += 1;
1054:       if (segment_selector_size != 0)
1055: 	{
1056: 	  _bfd_error_handler
1057: 	    (_("DWARF error: line info unsupported segment selector size %u"),
1058: 	     segment_selector_size);
1059: 	  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1060: 	  return NULL;
1061: 	}
1062:     }
1063: 
1064:   if (offset_size == 4)
1065:     lh.prologue_length = read_4_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1066:   else
1067:     lh.prologue_length = read_8_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1068:   line_ptr += offset_size;
1069: 
1070:   lh.minimum_instruction_length = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1071:   line_ptr += 1;
1072: 
1073:   if (lh.version >= 4)
1074:     {
1075:       lh.maximum_ops_per_insn = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1076:       line_ptr += 1;
1077:     }
1078:   else
1079:     lh.maximum_ops_per_insn = 1;
1080: 
1081:   if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 0)
1082:     {
1083:       _bfd_error_handler
1084: 	(_("DWARF error: invalid maximum operations per instruction"));
1085:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1086:       return NULL;
1087:     }
1088: 
1089:   lh.default_is_stmt = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1090:   line_ptr += 1;
1091: 
1092:   lh.line_base = read_1_signed_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1093:   line_ptr += 1;
1094: 
1095:   lh.line_range = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1096:   line_ptr += 1;
1097: 
1098:   lh.opcode_base = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1099:   line_ptr += 1;
1100: 
1101:   if (line_ptr + (lh.opcode_base - 1) >= line_end)
1102:     {
1103:       _bfd_error_handler (_("DWARF error: ran out of room reading opcodes"));
1104:       bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1105:       return NULL;
1106:     }
1107: 
1108:   amt = lh.opcode_base * sizeof (unsigned char);
1109:   lh.standard_opcode_lengths = (unsigned char *) bfd_alloc (abfd, amt);
1110: 
1111:   lh.standard_opcode_lengths[0] = 1;
1112: 
1113:   for (i = 1; i < lh.opcode_base; ++i)
1114:     {
1115:       lh.standard_opcode_lengths[i] = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1116:       line_ptr += 1;
1117:     }
1118: 
1119:   if (lh.version >= 5)
1120:     {
1121:       /* Read directory table.  */
1122:       if (!read_formatted_entries (unit, &line_ptr, line_end, table,
1123: 				   line_info_add_include_dir_stub))
1124: 	goto fail;
1125: 
1126:       /* Read file name table.  */
1127:       if (!read_formatted_entries (unit, &line_ptr, line_end, table,
1128: 				   line_info_add_file_name))
1129: 	goto fail;
1130:     }
1131:   else
1132:     {
1133:       /* Read directory table.  */
1134:       while ((cur_dir = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read)) != NULL)
1135: 	{
1136: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1137: 
1138: 	  if (!line_info_add_include_dir (table, cur_dir))
1139: 	    goto fail;
1140: 	}
1141: 
1142:       line_ptr += bytes_read;
1143: 
1144:       /* Read file name table.  */
1145:       while ((cur_file = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read)) != NULL)
1146: 	{
1147: 	  unsigned int dir, xtime, size;
1148: 
1149: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1150: 
1151: 	  dir = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1152: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1153: 	  xtime = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1154: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1155: 	  size = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read, FALSE, line_end);
1156: 	  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1157: 
1158: 	  if (!line_info_add_file_name (table, cur_file, dir, xtime, size))
1159: 	    goto fail;
1160: 	}
1161: 
1162:       line_ptr += bytes_read;
1163:     }
1164: 
1165:   /* Read the statement sequences until there's nothing left.  */
1166:   while (line_ptr < line_end)
1167:     {
1168:       /* State machine registers.  */
1169:       bfd_vma address = 0;
1170:       unsigned char op_index = 0;
1171:       char * filename = table->num_files ? concat_filename (table, 1) : NULL;
1172:       unsigned int line = 1;
1173:       unsigned int column = 0;
1174:       unsigned int discriminator = 0;
1175:       int is_stmt = lh.default_is_stmt;
1176:       int end_sequence = 0;
1177:       unsigned int dir, xtime, size;
1178:       /* eraxxon@alumni.rice.edu: Against the DWARF2 specs, some
1179: 	 compilers generate address sequences that are wildly out of
1180: 	 order using DW_LNE_set_address (e.g. Intel C++ 6.0 compiler
1181: 	 for ia64-Linux).  Thus, to determine the low and high
1182: 	 address, we must compare on every DW_LNS_copy, etc.  */
1183:       bfd_vma low_pc  = (bfd_vma) -1;
1184:       bfd_vma high_pc = 0;
1185: 
1186:       /* Decode the table.  */
1187:       while (!end_sequence && line_ptr < line_end)
1188: 	{
1189: 	  op_code = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1190: 	  line_ptr += 1;
1191: 
1192: 	  if (op_code >= lh.opcode_base)
1193: 	    {
1194: 	      /* Special operand.  */
1195: 	      adj_opcode = op_code - lh.opcode_base;
1196: 	      if (lh.line_range == 0)
1197: 		goto line_fail;
1198: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1199: 		address += (adj_opcode / lh.line_range
1200: 			    * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1201: 	      else
1202: 		{
1203: 		  address += ((op_index + adj_opcode / lh.line_range)
1204: 			      / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn
1205: 			      * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1206: 		  op_index = ((op_index + adj_opcode / lh.line_range)
1207: 			      % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn);
1208: 		}
1209: 	      line += lh.line_base + (adj_opcode % lh.line_range);
1210: 	      /* Append row to matrix using current values.  */
1211: 	      if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index, filename,
1212: 				  line, column, discriminator, 0))
1213: 		goto line_fail;
1214: 	      discriminator = 0;
1215: 	      if (address < low_pc)
1216: 		low_pc = address;
1217: 	      if (address > high_pc)
1218: 		high_pc = address;
1219: 	    }
1220: 	  else switch (op_code)
1221: 	    {
1222: 	    case DW_LNS_extended_op:
1223: 	      exop_len = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1224: 						FALSE, line_end);
1225: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1226: 	      extended_op = read_1_byte (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1227: 	      line_ptr += 1;
1228: 
1229: 	      switch (extended_op)
1230: 		{
1231: 		case DW_LNE_end_sequence:
1232: 		  end_sequence = 1;
1233: 		  if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index, filename, line,
1234: 				      column, discriminator, end_sequence))
1235: 		    goto line_fail;
1236: 		  discriminator = 0;
1237: 		  if (address < low_pc)
1238: 		    low_pc = address;
1239: 		  if (address > high_pc)
1240: 		    high_pc = address;
1241: 		  if (!arange_add (unit, &unit->arange, low_pc, high_pc))
1242: 		    goto line_fail;
1243: 		  break;
1244: 		case DW_LNE_set_address:
1245: 		  address = read_address (unit, line_ptr, line_end);
1246: 		  op_index = 0;
1247: 		  line_ptr += unit->addr_size;
1248: 		  break;
1249: 		case DW_LNE_define_file:
1250: 		  cur_file = read_string (abfd, line_ptr, line_end, &bytes_read);
1251: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1252: 		  dir = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1253: 					       FALSE, line_end);
1254: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1255: 		  xtime = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1256: 						 FALSE, line_end);
1257: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1258: 		  size = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1259: 						FALSE, line_end);
1260: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1261: 		  if (!line_info_add_file_name (table, cur_file, dir,
1262: 						xtime, size))
1263: 		    goto line_fail;
1264: 		  break;
1265: 		case DW_LNE_set_discriminator:
1266: 		  discriminator =
1267: 		    _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1268: 					   FALSE, line_end);
1269: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1270: 		  break;
1271: 		case DW_LNE_HP_source_file_correlation:
1272: 		  line_ptr += exop_len - 1;
1273: 		  break;
1274: 		default:
1275: 		  _bfd_error_handler
1276: 		    (_("DWARF error: mangled line number section"));
1277: 		  bfd_set_error (bfd_error_bad_value);
1278: 		line_fail:
1279: 		  if (filename != NULL)
1280: 		    free (filename);
1281: 		  goto fail;
1282: 		}
1283: 	      break;
1284: 	    case DW_LNS_copy:
1285: 	      if (!add_line_info (table, address, op_index,
1286: 				  filename, line, column, discriminator, 0))
1287: 		goto line_fail;
1288: 	      discriminator = 0;
1289: 	      if (address < low_pc)
1290: 		low_pc = address;
1291: 	      if (address > high_pc)
1292: 		high_pc = address;
1293: 	      break;
1294: 	    case DW_LNS_advance_pc:
1295: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1296: 		address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1297: 			    * _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr,
1298: 						     &bytes_read,
1299: 						     FALSE, line_end));
1300: 	      else
1301: 		{
1302: 		  bfd_vma adjust = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr,
1303: 							  &bytes_read,
1304: 							  FALSE, line_end);
1305: 		  address = ((op_index + adjust) / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn
1306: 			     * lh.minimum_instruction_length);
1307: 		  op_index = (op_index + adjust) % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn;
1308: 		}
1309: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1310: 	      break;
1311: 	    case DW_LNS_advance_line:
1312: 	      line += _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1313: 					     TRUE, line_end);
1314: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1315: 	      break;
1316: 	    case DW_LNS_set_file:
1317: 	      {
1318: 		unsigned int file;
1319: 
1320: 		/* The file and directory tables are 0
1321: 		   based, the references are 1 based.  */
1322: 		file = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1323: 					      FALSE, line_end);
1324: 		line_ptr += bytes_read;
1325: 		if (filename)
1326: 		  free (filename);
1327: 		filename = concat_filename (table, file);
1328: 		break;
1329: 	      }
1330: 	    case DW_LNS_set_column:
1331: 	      column = _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1332: 					      FALSE, line_end);
1333: 	      line_ptr += bytes_read;
1334: 	      break;
1335: 	    case DW_LNS_negate_stmt:
1336: 	      is_stmt = (!is_stmt);
1337: 	      break;
1338: 	    case DW_LNS_set_basic_block:
1339: 	      break;
1340: 	    case DW_LNS_const_add_pc:
1341: 	      if (lh.line_range == 0)
1342: 		goto line_fail;
1343: 	      if (lh.maximum_ops_per_insn == 1)
1344: 		address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1345: 			    * ((255 - lh.opcode_base) / lh.line_range));
1346: 	      else
1347: 		{
1348: 		  bfd_vma adjust = ((255 - lh.opcode_base) / lh.line_range);
1349: 		  address += (lh.minimum_instruction_length
1350: 			      * ((op_index + adjust)
1351: 				 / lh.maximum_ops_per_insn));
1352: 		  op_index = (op_index + adjust) % lh.maximum_ops_per_insn;
1353: 		}
1354: 	      break;
1355: 	    case DW_LNS_fixed_advance_pc:
1356: 	      address += read_2_bytes (abfd, line_ptr, line_end);
1357: 	      op_index = 0;
1358: 	      line_ptr += 2;
1359: 	      break;
1360: 	    default:
1361: 	      /* Unknown standard opcode, ignore it.  */
1362: 	      for (i = 0; i < lh.standard_opcode_lengths[op_code]; i++)
1363: 		{
1364: 		  (void) _bfd_safe_read_leb128 (abfd, line_ptr, &bytes_read,
1365: 						FALSE, line_end);
1366: 		  line_ptr += bytes_read;
1367: 		}
1368: 	      break;
1369: 	    }
1370: 	}
1371: 
1372:       if (filename)
1373: 	free (filename);
1374:     }
1375: 
1376:   if (sort_line_sequences (table))
1377:     return table;
1378: 
1379:  fail:
1380:   while (table->sequences != NULL)
1381:     {
1382:       struct line_sequence* seq = table->sequences;
1383:       table->sequences = table->sequences->prev_sequence;
1384:       free (seq);
1385:     }
1386:   if (table->files != NULL)
1387:     free (table->files);
1388:   if (table->dirs != NULL)
1389:     free (table->dirs);
1390:   return NULL;
1391: }

1393: static bfd_vma
1394: comp_unit_find_nearest_line (struct comp_unit *unit,
1395: 			     bfd_vma addr,
1396: 			     const char **filename_ptr,
1397: 			     struct funcinfo **function_ptr,
1398: 			     unsigned int *linenumber_ptr,
1399: 			     unsigned int *discriminator_ptr,
1400: 			     struct dwarf2_debug *stash)
1401: {
1402:   bfd_boolean func_p;
1403: 
1404:   if (unit->error)
1405:     return FALSE;
1406: 
1407:   if (! unit->line_table)
1408:     {
1409:       if (! unit->stmtlist)
1410: 	{
1411: 	  unit->error = 1;
1412: 	  return FALSE;
1413: 	}
1414: 
1415:       unit->line_table = decode_line_info (unit, stash);
1416: 
1417:       if (! unit->line_table)
1418: 	{
1419: 	  unit->error = 1;
1420: 	  return FALSE;
1421: 	}
1422: 
1423:       if (unit->first_child_die_ptr < unit->end_ptr
1424: 	  && ! scan_unit_for_symbols (unit))
1425: 	{
1426: 	  unit->error = 1;
1427: 	  return FALSE;
1428: 	}
1429:     }
1430: 
1431:   *function_ptr = NULL;
1432:   func_p = lookup_address_in_function_table (unit, addr, function_ptr);
1433:   if (func_p && (*function_ptr)->tag == DW_TAG_inlined_subroutine)
1434:     stash->inliner_chain = *function_ptr;
1435: 
1436:   return lookup_address_in_line_info_table (unit->line_table, addr,
1437: 					    filename_ptr,
1438: 					    linenumber_ptr,
1439: 					    discriminator_ptr);
1440: }

1443: static char *
1444: concat_filename (struct line_info_table *table, unsigned int file)
1445: {
1446:   char *filename;
1447: 
1448:   if (table == NULL || file - 1 >= table->num_files)
1449:     {
1450:       /* FILE == 0 means unknown.  */
1451:       if (file)
1452: 	_bfd_error_handler
1453: 	  (_("DWARF error: mangled line number section (bad file number)"));
1454:       return strdup ("<unknown>");
1455:     }
1456: 
1457:   filename = table->files[file - 1].name;
1458:   if (filename == NULL)
1459:     return strdup ("<unknown>");
1460: 
1461:   if (!IS_ABSOLUTE_PATH (filename))
1462:     {
1463:       char *dir_name = NULL;
1464:       char *subdir_name = NULL;
1465:       char *name;
1466:       size_t len;
1467: 
1468:       if (table->files[file - 1].dir
1469: 	  /* PR 17512: file: 0317e960.  */
1470: 	  && table->files[file - 1].dir <= table->num_dirs
1471: 	  /* PR 17512: file: 7f3d2e4b.  */
1472: 	  && table->dirs != NULL)
1473: 	subdir_name = table->dirs[table->files[file - 1].dir - 1];
1474: 
1475:       if (!subdir_name || !IS_ABSOLUTE_PATH (subdir_name))
1476: 	dir_name = table->comp_dir;
1477: 
1478:       if (!dir_name)
1479: 	{
1480: 	  dir_name = subdir_name;
1481: 	  subdir_name = NULL;
1482: 	}
1483: 
1484:       if (!dir_name)
1485: 	return strdup (filename);
1486: 
1487:       len = strlen (dir_name) + strlen (filename) + 2;
1488: 
1489:       if (subdir_name)
1490: 	{
1491: 	  len += strlen (subdir_name) + 1;
1492: 	  name = (char *) bfd_malloc (len);
1493: 	  if (name)
1494: 	    sprintf (name, "%s/%s/%s", dir_name, subdir_name, filename);
1495: 	}
1496:       else
1497: 	{
1498: 	  name = (char *) bfd_malloc (len);
1499: 	  if (name)
1500: 	    sprintf (name, "%s/%s", dir_name, filename);
1501: 	}
1502: 
1503:       return name;
1504:     }
1505: 
1506:   return strdup (filename);
1507: }

1509: static bfd_boolean
1510: find_abstract_instance (struct comp_unit *   unit,
1511: 			bfd_byte *           orig_info_ptr,
1512: 			struct attribute *   attr_ptr,
1513: 			const char **        pname,
1514: 			bfd_boolean *        is_linkage,
1515: 			char **              filename_ptr,
1516: 			int *                linenumber_ptr)
1517: {
1518:   bfd *abfd = unit->abfd;
1519:   bfd_byte *info_ptr;
1520:   bfd_byte *info_ptr_end;

1890: 		  if (is_str_attr (attr.form))
1891: 		    var->name = attr.u.str;
1892: 		  break;
1893: 
1894: 		case DW_AT_decl_file:
1895: 		  var->file = concat_filename (unit->line_table,
1896: 					       attr.u.val);
1897: 		  break;
1898: 
1899: 		case DW_AT_decl_line:
1900: 		  var->line = attr.u.val;
1901: 		  break;
1902: 
1903: 		case DW_AT_external:
1904: 		  if (attr.u.val != 0)
1905: 		    var->stack = 0;
1906: 		  break;
1907: 
1908: 		case DW_AT_location:
1909: 		  switch (attr.form)
1910: 		    {
1911: 		    case DW_FORM_block:
1912: 		    case DW_FORM_block1:
1913: 		    case DW_FORM_block2:
1914: 		    case DW_FORM_block4:
1915: 		    case DW_FORM_exprloc:
1916: 		      if (attr.u.blk->data != NULL
1917: 			  && *attr.u.blk->data == DW_OP_addr)
1918: 			{
1919: 			  var->stack = 0;
1920: 
1921: 			  /* Verify that DW_OP_addr is the only opcode in the
1922: 			     location, in which case the block size will be 1
1923: 			     plus the address size.  */
1924: 			  /* ??? For TLS variables, gcc can emit
1925: 			     DW_OP_addr <addr> DW_OP_GNU_push_tls_address
1926: 			     which we don't handle here yet.  */
1927: 			  if (attr.u.blk->size == unit->addr_size + 1U)
1928: 			    var->addr = bfd_get (unit->abfd, unit->addr_size * 8,
1929: 						 attr.u.blk->data + 1);
1930: 			}
1931: 		      break;
1932: 
1933: 		    default:
1934: 		      break;
1935: 		    }
1936: 		  break;
1937: 
1938: 		default:
1939: 		  break;
1940: 		}
1941: 	    }
1942: 	}
1943: 
1944:       if (high_pc_relative)
1945: 	high_pc += low_pc;
1946: 
1947:       if (func && high_pc != 0)
1948: 	{
1949: 	  if (!arange_add (unit, &func->arange, low_pc, high_pc))
1950: 	    goto fail;
1951: 	}
1952: 
1953:       if (abbrev->has_children)
1954: 	{
1955: 	  nesting_level++;
1956: 
1957: 	  if (nesting_level >= nested_funcs_size)
1958: 	    {
1959: 	      struct nest_funcinfo *tmp;
1960: 
1961: 	      nested_funcs_size *= 2;
1962: 	      tmp = (struct nest_funcinfo *)
1963: 		bfd_realloc (nested_funcs,
1964: 			     nested_funcs_size * sizeof (*nested_funcs));
1965: 	      if (tmp == NULL)
1966: 		goto fail;
1967: 	      nested_funcs = tmp;
1968: 	    }
1969: 	  nested_funcs[nesting_level].func = 0;
1970: 	}
1971:     }
1972: 
1973:   free (nested_funcs);
1974:   return TRUE;
1975: 
1976:  fail:
1977:   free (nested_funcs);
1978:   return FALSE;
1979: }

1981: static bfd_boolean
1982: comp_unit_maybe_decode_line_info (struct comp_unit *unit,
1983: 				  struct dwarf2_debug *stash)
1984: {
1985:   if (unit->error)
1986:     return FALSE;
1987: 
1988:   if (! unit->line_table)
1989:     {
1990:       if (! unit->stmtlist)
1991: 	{
1992: 	  unit->error = 1;
1993: 	  return FALSE;
1994: 	}
1995: 
1996:       unit->line_table = decode_line_info (unit, stash);
1997: 
1998:       if (! unit->line_table)
1999: 	{
2000: 	  unit->error = 1;
2001: 	  return FALSE;
2002: 	}
2003: 
2004:       if (unit->first_child_die_ptr < unit->end_ptr
2005: 	  && ! scan_unit_for_symbols (unit))
2006: 	{
2007: 	  unit->error = 1;
2008: 	  return FALSE;
2009: 	}
2010:     }
2011: 
2012:   return TRUE;
2013: }

2015: static bfd_boolean
2016: comp_unit_find_line (struct comp_unit *unit,
2017: 		     asymbol *sym,
2018: 		     bfd_vma addr,
2019: 		     const char **filename_ptr,
2020: 		     unsigned int *linenumber_ptr,
2021: 		     struct dwarf2_debug *stash)
2022: {
2023:   if (!comp_unit_maybe_decode_line_info (unit, stash))
2024:     return FALSE;
2025: 
2026:   if (sym->flags & BSF_FUNCTION)
2027:     return lookup_symbol_in_function_table (unit, sym, addr,
2028: 					    filename_ptr,
2029: 					    linenumber_ptr);
2030: 
2031:   return lookup_symbol_in_variable_table (unit, sym, addr,
2032: 					  filename_ptr,
2033: 					  linenumber_ptr);
2034: }
